entries.answer: The sacraments of baptism and the Lord's supper differ, in that baptism is to be administered but once, with water, to be a sign and seal of our regeneration and ingrafting into Christ, and that even to infants; whereas the Lord's supper is to be administered often, in the elements of bread and wine, to represent and exhibit Christ as spiritual nourishment to the soul, and to confirm our continuance and growth in him, and that only to such as are of years and ability to examine themselves.
entries.question_mesv: How do the sacraments of baptism and the Lord's Supper differ?
entries.answer_mesv: The sacraments of baptism and the Lord's Supper differ, in that baptism is to be administered only once, with water, to be a sign and seal of our regeneration and ingrafting into Christ, and that even to infants; but the Lord's Supper is to be administered often, in the elements of bread and wine, to represent and exhibit4 Christ as spiritual nourishment to the soul, and to confirm our continuance and growth in him, and that only to those who are of years and ability to examine themselves.

entries.question: What is prayer?
entries.answer: Prayer is an offering up of our desires unto God, in the name of Christ, by the help of his Spirit; with confession of our sins, and thankful acknowledgement of his mercies.
entries.question_mesv: What is prayer?
entries.answer_mesv: Prayer is an offering up of our desires to God, in the name of Christ, by the help of his Spirit; with confession of our sins, and thankful acknowledgment of his mercies.

entries.question: Are we to pray unto God only?
entries.answer: God only being able to search the hearts, hear the requests, pardon the sins, and fulfill the desires of all; and only to be believed in, and worshiped with religious worship; prayer, which is a special part thereof, is to be made by all to him alone, and to none other.
entries.question_mesv: Are we to pray to God only?
entries.answer_mesv: God only being able to search the hearts, hear the requests, pardon the sins, and fulfill the desires of all; and only to be believed in, and worshiped with religious worship; prayer, which is a special part of it, is to be made by all to him alone, and to none other.

entries.question: What is it to pray in the name of Christ?
entries.answer: To pray in the name of Christ is, in obedience to his command, and in confidence on his promises, to ask mercy for his sake; not by bare mentioning of his name, but by drawing our encouragement to pray, and our boldness, strength, and hope of acceptance in prayer, from Christ and his mediation.
entries.question_mesv: What is it to pray in the name of Christ?
entries.answer_mesv: To pray in the name of Christ is, in obedience to his command, and in confidence on his promises, to ask mercy for his sake; not by bare mentioning of his name, but by drawing our encouragement to pray, and our boldness, strength, and hope of acceptance in prayer, from Christ and his mediation.

entries.question: Why are we to pray in the name of Christ?
entries.answer: The sinfulness of man, and his distance from God by reason thereof, being so great, as that we can have no access into his presence without a mediator; and there being none in heaven or earth appointed to, or fit for, that glorious work but Christ alone, we are to pray in no other name but his only.
entries.question_mesv: Why are we to pray in the name of Christ?
entries.answer_mesv: The sinfulness of man, and, for that reason, his distance from God being so great, that we can have no access into his presence without a mediator; and there being none in heaven or earth appointed to, or fit for, that glorious work but Christ alone, we are to pray in no other name but his only.

entries.question: How doth the Spirit help us to pray?
entries.answer: We not knowing what to pray for as we ought, the Spirit helpeth our infirmities, by enabling us to understand both for whom, and what, and how prayer is to be made; and by working and quickening in our hearts (although not in all persons, nor at all times, in the same measure) those apprehensions, affections, and graces which are requisite for the right performance of that duty.
entries.question_mesv: How does the Spirit help us to pray?
entries.answer_mesv: We not knowing what to pray for as we ought, the Spirit helps our infirmities, by enabling us to understand both for whom, and what, and how prayer is to be made; and by working and making alive in our hearts (although not in all persons, nor at all times, in the same measure) those apprehensions, affections, and graces that are requisite for the right performance of that duty. 4 The word exhibit suggests that the grace signified and sealed by the elements of the sacrament is truly communicated and applied to worshippers who partake of them by faith.

entries.question: For whom are we to pray?
entries.answer: We are to pray for the whole church of Christ upon earth; for magistrates, and ministers; for ourselves, our brethren, yea, our enemies; and for all sorts of men living, or that shall live hereafter; but not for the dead, nor for those that are known to have sinned the sin unto death.
entries.question_mesv: For whom are we to pray?
entries.answer_mesv: We are to pray for the whole church of Christ upon earth; for magistrates, and ministers; for ourselves, our brothers, and even our enemies; and for all sorts of men living, or that shall yet live; but not for the dead, nor for those that are known to have sinned the sin that leads to death.

entries.question: For what things are we to pray?
entries.answer: We are to pray for all things tending to the glory of God, the welfare of the church, our own or others' good; but not for anything that is unlawful.
entries.question_mesv: For what things are we to pray?
entries.answer_mesv: We are to pray for all things tending to the glory of God, the welfare of the church, our own or others' good; but not for anything that is unlawful.

entries.question: How are we to pray?
entries.answer: We are to pray with an awful apprehension of the majesty of God, and deep sense of our own unworthiness, necessities, and sins; with penitent, thankful, and enlarged hearts; with understanding, faith, sincerity, fervency, love, and perseverance, waiting upon him, with humble submission to his will.
entries.question_mesv: How are we to pray?
entries.answer_mesv: We are to pray with an awe-filled apprehension of the majesty of God, and deep sense of our own unworthiness, necessities, and sins; with penitent, thankful, and enlarged hearts; with understanding, faith, sincerity, fervency, love, and perseverance, waiting upon him, with humble submission to his will.

entries.question: What rule hath God given for our direction in the duty of prayer?
entries.answer: The whole Word of God is of use to direct us in the duty of prayer; but the special rule of direction is that form of prayer which our Savior Christ taught his disciples, commonly called The Lord's prayer.
entries.question_mesv: What rule has God given for our direction in the duty of prayer?
entries.answer_mesv: The whole Word of God is of use to direct us in the duty of prayer; but the special rule of direction is that form of prayer which our Savior Christ taught his disciples, commonly called the Lord's Prayer.

entries.question: How is the Lord's prayer to be used?
entries.answer: The Lord's prayer is not only for direction, as a pattern, according to which we are to make other prayers; but may also be used as a prayer, so that it be done with understanding, faith, reverence, and other graces necessary to the right performance of the duty of prayer.
entries.question_mesv: How is the Lord's Prayer to be used?
entries.answer_mesv: The Lord's Prayer is not only for direction, as a pattern, according to which we are to make other prayers; but may also be used as a prayer, provided it is done with understanding, faith, reverence, and other graces necessary to the right performance of the duty of prayer.

entries.question: Of how many parts doth the Lord's prayer consist?
entries.answer: The Lord's prayer consists of three parts; a preface, petitions, and a conclusion.
entries.question_mesv: Of how many parts does the Lord's Prayer consist?
entries.answer_mesv: The Lord's Prayer consists of three parts; a preface, petitions, and a conclusion.

entries.question: What doth the preface of the Lord's prayer teach us?
entries.answer: The preface of the Lord's prayer (contained in these words, Our Father which art in heaven) teacheth us, when we pray, to draw near to God with confidence of his fatherly goodness, and our interest therein; with reverence, and all other childlike dispositions, heavenly affections, and due apprehensions of his sovereign power, majesty, and gracious condescension: as also, to pray with and for others.
entries.question_mesv: What does the preface of the Lord's Prayer teach us?
entries.answer_mesv: The preface of the Lord's Prayer (contained in these words, Our Father in heaven,) teaches us, when we pray, to draw near to God with confidence in his fatherly goodness, and our interest in it; with reverence, and all other childlike dispositions, heavenly affections, and due apprehensions of his sovereign power, majesty, and gracious condescension: and also, to pray with and for others.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the first petition?
entries.answer: In the first petition (which is, Hallowed be thy name), acknowledging the utter inability and indisposition that is in ourselves and all men to honor God aright, we pray, that God would by his grace enable and incline us and others to know, to acknowledge, and highly to esteem him, his titles, attributes, ordinances, word, works, and whatsoever he is pleased to make himself known by; and to glorify him in thought, word, and deed: that he would prevent and remove atheism, ignorance, idolatry, profaneness, and whatsoever is dishonorable to him; and, by his overruling providence, direct and dispose of all things to his own glory.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the first petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the first petition, (which is, Hallowed be your name,) acknowledging the utter inability and indisposition that is in ourselves and all men to honor God rightly, we pray, that God would by his grace enable and incline us and others to know, to acknowledge, and highly to esteem him, his titles, attributes, ordinances, Word, works, and whatever he is pleased to make himself known by; and to glorify him in thought, word, and deed: that he would prevent and remove atheism, ignorance, idolatry, profaneness, and whatever is dishonorable to him; and, by his overruling providence, direct and order all things to his own glory.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the second petition?
entries.answer: In the second petition (which is, Thy kingdom come), acknowledging ourselves and all mankind to be by nature under the dominion of sin and Satan, we pray, that the kingdom of sin and Satan may be destroyed, the gospel propagated throughout the world, the Jews called, the fullness of the Gentiles brought in; the church furnished with all gospel officers and ordinances, purged from corruption, countenanced and maintained by the civil magistrate; that the ordinances of Christ may be purely dispensed, and made effectual to the converting of those that are yet in their sins, and the confirming, comforting, and building up of those that are already converted: that Christ would rule in our hearts here, and hasten the time of his second coming, and our reigning with him forever: and that he would be pleased so to exercise the kingdom of his power in all the world, as may best conduce to these ends.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the second petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the second petition, (which is, Your kingdom come,) acknowledging ourselves and all mankind to be by nature under the dominion of sin and Satan, we pray, that the kingdom of sin and Satan may be destroyed, the gospel propagated throughout the world, the Jews called, the fullness of the Gentiles brought in; the church furnished with all gospel officers and ordinances, purged from corruption, countenanced and maintained by the civil magistrate: that the ordinances of Christ may be purely dispensed, and made effectual to the converting of those that are yet in their sins, and the confirming, comforting, and building up of those that are already converted: that Christ would rule in our hearts here, and hasten the time of his second coming, and our reigning with him forever: and that he would be pleased so to exercise the kingdom of his power in all the world, as may best conduce to these ends.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the third petition?
entries.answer: In the third petition (which is, Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven), acknowledging that by nature we and all men are not only utterly unable and unwilling to know and to do the will of God, but prone to rebel against his word, to repine and murmur against his providence, and wholly inclined to do the will of the flesh, and of the devil: we pray, that God would by his Spirit take away from ourselves and others all blindness, weakness, indisposedness, and perverseness of heart; and by his grace make us able and willing to know, do, and submit to his will in all things, with the like humility, cheerfulness, faithfulness, diligence, zeal, sincerity, and constancy, as the angels do in heaven.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the third petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the third petition, (which is, Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven,) acknowledging that by nature we and all men are not only utterly unable and unwilling to know and do the will of God, but prone to rebel against his Word, to grumble and murmur against his providence, and wholly inclined to do the will of the flesh, and of the devil: we pray, that God would by his Spirit take away from ourselves and others all blindness, weakness, unwillingness, and perverseness of heart; and by his grace make us able and willing to know, do, and submit to his will in all things, with the same humility, cheerfulness, faithfulness, diligence, zeal, sincerity, and constancy, as the angels do in heaven.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the fourth petition?
entries.answer: In the fourth petition (which is, Give us this day our daily bread), acknowledging that in Adam, and by our own sin, we have forfeited our right to all the outward blessings of this life, and deserve to be wholly deprived of them by God, and to have them cursed to us in the use of them; and that neither they of themselves are able to sustain us, nor we to merit, or by our own industry to procure them; but prone to desire, get, and use them unlawfully: we pray for ourselves and others, that both they and we, waiting upon the providence of God from day to day in the use of lawful means, may, of his free gift, and as to his fatherly wisdom shall seem best, enjoy a competent portion of them; and have the same continued and blessed unto us in our holy and comfortable use of them, and contentment in them; and be kept from all things that are contrary to our temporal support and comfort.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the fourth petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the fourth petition, (which is, Give us this day our daily bread,) acknowledging that in Adam, and by our own sin, we have forfeited our right to all the outward blessings of this life, and deserve to be wholly deprived of them by God, and to have them cursed to us in the use of them; and that neither they of themselves are able to sustain us, nor we to merit, or by our own industry to procure them; but prone to desire, get, and use them unlawfully: we pray for ourselves and others, that both they and we, waiting upon the providence of God from day to day in the use of lawful means, may, of his free gift, and as to his fatherly wisdom shall seem best, enjoy a fitting portion of them; and have the same continued and blessed to us in our holy and comfortable use of them, and contentment in them; and be kept from all things that are contrary to our temporal support and comfort.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the fifth petition?
entries.answer: In the fifth petition (which is, Forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors), acknowledging that we and all others are guilty both of original and actual sin, and thereby become debtors to the justice of God; and that neither we, nor any other creature, can make the least satisfaction for that debt: we pray for ourselves and others, that God of his free grace would, through the obedience and satisfaction of Christ, apprehended and applied by faith, acquit us both from the guilt and punishment of sin, accept us in his Beloved; continue his favor and grace to us, pardon our daily failings, and fill us with peace and joy, in giving us daily more and more assurance of forgiveness; which we are the rather emboldened to ask, and encouraged to expect, when we have this testimony in ourselves, that we from the heart forgive others their offenses.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the fifth petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the fifth petition, (which is, And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors,) acknowledging that we and all others are guilty both of original and actual sin, and by these become debtors to the justice of God; and that neither we, nor any other creature, can make the least satisfaction for that debt: we pray for ourselves and others, that God of his free grace would, through the obedience and satisfaction of Christ, apprehended and applied by faith, acquit us both from the guilt and punishment of sin, accept us in his Beloved; continue his favor and grace to us, pardon our daily failings, and fill us with peace and joy, in giving us daily more and more assurance of forgiveness; which we are even more emboldened to ask and encouraged to expect, when we have this testimony in ourselves, that we from the heart forgive others their offenses.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the sixth petition?
entries.answer: In the sixth petition (which is, And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil), acknowledging that the most wise, righteous, and gracious God, for divers holy and just ends, may so order things, that we may be assaulted, foiled, and for a time led captive by temptations; that Satan, the world, and the flesh, are ready powerfully to draw us aside, and ensnare us; and that we, even after the pardon of our sins, by reason of our corruption, weakness, and want of watchfulness, are not only subject to be tempted, and forward to expose ourselves unto temptations, but also of ourselves unable and unwilling to resist them, to recover out of them, and to improve them; and worthy to be left under the power of them; we pray, that God would so overrule the world and all in it, subdue the flesh, and restrain Satan, order all things, bestow and bless all means of grace, and quicken us to watchfulness in the use of them, that we and all his people may by his providence be kept from being tempted to sin; or, if tempted, that by his Spirit we may be powerfully supported and enabled to stand in the hour of temptation; or when fallen, raised again and recovered out of it, and have a sanctified use and improvement thereof: that our sanctification and salvation may be perfected, Satan trodden under our feet, and we fully freed from sin, temptation, and all evil, forever.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the sixth petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the sixth petition, (which is, And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil,) acknowledging that the most wise, righteous, and gracious God, for various holy and just ends, may so order things, that we may be assaulted, foiled, and for a time led captive by temptations; that Satan, the world, and the flesh, are ready powerfully to draw us aside, and ensnare us; and that we, even after the pardon of our sins, by reason of our corruption, weakness, and lack of watchfulness, are not only subject to be tempted, and forward to expose ourselves to temptations, but also of ourselves unable and unwilling to resist them, to recover out of them, and to make good use of them; and worthy to be left under the power of them: we pray, that God would so overrule the world and all in it, subdue the flesh, and restrain Satan, order all things, bestow and bless all means of grace, and revive us to watchfulness in the use of them, that we and all his people may by his providence be kept from being tempted to sin; or, if tempted, that by his Spirit we may be powerfully supported and enabled to stand in the hour of temptation; or when fallen, raised again and recovered out of it, and have a sanctified and profitable use of it: that our sanctification and salvation may be perfected, Satan trodden under our feet, and we fully freed from sin, temptation, and all evil, forever.

entries.question: What doth the conclusion of the Lord's prayer teach us?
entries.answer: The conclusion of the Lord's prayer (which is, For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever. Amen.) teacheth us to enforce our petitions with arguments, which are to be taken, not from any worthiness in ourselves, or in any other creature, but from God; and with our prayers to join praises, ascribing to God alone eternal sovereignty, omnipotency, and glorious excellency; in regard whereof, as he is able and willing to help us, so we by faith are emboldened to plead with him that he would, and quietly to rely upon him, that he will fulfill our requests. And, to testify this our desire and assurance, we say, Amen.
entries.question_mesv: What does the conclusion of the Lord's Prayer teach us?
entries.answer_mesv: The conclusion of the Lord's Prayer, (which is, For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory, forever. Amen.) teaches us to support our petitions with arguments, which are to be taken, not from any worthiness in ourselves, or in any other creature, but from God; and with our prayers to join praises, ascribing to God alone eternal sovereignty, omnipotence, and glorious excellency; in this regard, as he is able and willing to help us, so we by faith are emboldened to plead with him that he would, and quietly to rely upon him, that he will fulfill our requests. And, to testify this our desire and assurance, we say, Amen.

entries.question: What is the chief end of man?
entries.answer: Man's chief end is to glorify God, and to enjoy him forever.
entries.question_mesv: What is the chief end of man?
entries.answer_mesv: Man's chief end is to glorify God, and to enjoy him forever.

entries.question: What rule hath God given to direct us how we may glorify and enjoy him?
entries.answer: The Word of God, which is contained in the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments, is the only rule to direct us how we may glorify and enjoy him.
entries.question_mesv: What rule has God given to direct us how we may glorify and enjoy him?
entries.answer_mesv: The word of God, which is contained in the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments, is the only rule to direct us how we may glorify and enjoy him.

entries.question: What do the Scriptures principally teach?
entries.answer: The Scriptures principally teach what man is to believe concerning God, and what duty God requires of man.
entries.question_mesv: What do the Scriptures principally teach?
entries.answer_mesv: The Scriptures principally teach, what man is to believe concerning God, and what duty God requires of man.

entries.question: What is God?
entries.answer: God is a spirit, infinite, eternal, and unchangeable, in his being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness and truth.
entries.question_mesv: What is God?
entries.answer_mesv: God is a Spirit, infinite, eternal, and unchangeable, in his being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness, and truth.

entries.question: Are there more Gods than one?
entries.answer: There is but one only, the living and true God.
entries.question_mesv: Are there more Gods than one?
entries.answer_mesv: There is only one, the living and true God.

entries.question: How many persons are there in the godhead?
entries.answer: There are three persons in the Godhead; the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost; and these three are one God, the same in substance, equal in power and glory.
entries.question_mesv: How many persons are there in the Godhead?
entries.answer_mesv: There are three persons in the Godhead; the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit; and these three are one God, the same in substance, equal in power and glory.

entries.question: What are the decrees of God?
entries.answer: The decrees of God are his eternal purpose, according to the counsel of his will, whereby, for his own glory, he hath foreordained whatsoever comes to pass.
entries.question_mesv: What are the decrees of God?
entries.answer_mesv: The decrees of God are, his eternal purpose, according to the counsel of his will, by which, for his own glory, he has foreordained whatever comes to pass.

entries.question: How doth God execute his decrees?
entries.answer: God executeth his decrees in the works of creation and providence.
entries.question_mesv: How does God execute his decrees?
entries.answer_mesv: God executes his decrees in the works of creation and providence.

entries.question: What is the work of creation?
entries.answer: The work of creation is God's making all things of nothing, by the word of his power, in the space of six days, and all very good.
entries.question_mesv: What is the work of creation?
entries.answer_mesv: The work of creation is, God's making all things of nothing, by the word of his power, in the space of six days, and all very good.

entries.question: How did God create man?
entries.answer: God created man male and female, after his own image, in knowledge, righteousness and holiness, with dominion over the creatures.
entries.question_mesv: How did God create man?
entries.answer_mesv: God created man male and female, after his own image, in knowledge, righteousness, and holiness, with dominion over the creatures.

entries.question: What are God's works of providence?
entries.answer: God's works of providence are his most holy, wise and powerful preserving and governing all his creatures, and all their actions.
entries.question_mesv: What are God's works of providence?
entries.answer_mesv: God's works of providence are his most holy, wise, and powerful preserving and governing all his creatures, and all their actions.

entries.question: What special act of providence did God exercise toward man in the estate wherein he was created?
entries.answer: When God had created man, he entered into a covenant of life with him, upon condition of perfect obedience; forbidding him to eat of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, upon pain of death.
entries.question_mesv: What special act of providence did God exercise towards man in the state in which he was created?
entries.answer_mesv: When God had created man, he entered into a covenant of life with him, upon condition of perfect obedience; forbidding him to eat of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, upon pain of death.

entries.question: Did our first parents continue in the estate wherein they were created?
entries.answer: Our first parents, being left to the freedom of their own will, fell from the estate wherein they were created, by sinning against God.
entries.question_mesv: Did our first parents continue in the state in which they were created?
entries.answer_mesv: Our first parents, being left to the freedom of their own will, fell from the state in which they were created, by sinning against God.

entries.question: What is sin?
entries.answer: Sin is any want of conformity unto, or transgression of, the law of God.
entries.question_mesv: What is sin?
entries.answer_mesv: Sin is any lack of conformity to, or transgression of, the law of God.

entries.question: What was the sin whereby our first parents fell from the estate wherein they were created?
entries.answer: The sin whereby our first parents fell from the estate wherein they were created was their eating the forbidden fruit.
entries.question_mesv: What was the sin by which our first parents fell from the state in which they were created?
entries.answer_mesv: The sin by which our first parents fell from the state in which they were created, was their eating the forbidden fruit.

entries.question: Did all mankind fall in Adam's first transgression?
entries.answer: The covenant being made with Adam, not only for himself, but for his posterity; all mankind, descending from him by ordinary generation, sinned in him, and fell with him, in his first transgression.
entries.question_mesv: Did all humanity fall in Adam's first transgression?
entries.answer_mesv: The covenant being made with Adam, not only for himself, but for his posterity; all humanity, descending from him by ordinary generation, sinned in him, and fell with him, in his first transgression.

entries.question: Into what estate did the fall bring mankind?
entries.answer: The fall brought mankind into an estate of sin and misery.
entries.question_mesv: Into what state did the fall bring humanity?
entries.answer_mesv: The fall brought humanity into a state of sin and misery.

entries.question: Wherein consists the sinfulness of that estate whereinto man fell?
entries.answer: The sinfulness of that estate whereinto man fell consists in the guilt of Adam's first sin, the want of original righteousness, and the corruption of his whole nature, which is commonly called original sin; together with all actual transgressions which proceed from it.
entries.question_mesv: What is the sinfulness of that state into which man fell?
entries.answer_mesv: The sinfulness of that state into which man fell, consists in the guilt of Adam's first sin, the lack of original righteousness, and the corruption of his whole nature, which is commonly called original sin; together with all actual transgressions that proceed from it.

entries.question: What is the misery of that estate whereinto man fell?
entries.answer: All mankind by their fall lost communion with God, are under his wrath and curse, and so made liable to all the miseries in this life, to death itself, and to the pains of hell forever.
entries.question_mesv: What is the misery of that state into which man fell?
entries.answer_mesv: All humanity by their fall lost communion with God, are under his wrath and curse, and so made liable to all the miseries of this life, to death itself, and to the pains of hell forever.

entries.question: Did God leave all mankind to perish in the estate of sin and misery?
entries.answer: God having, out of his mere good pleasure, from all eternity, elected some to everlasting life, did enter into a covenant of grace, to deliver them out of the estate of sin and misery, and to bring them into an estate of salvation by a redeemer.
entries.question_mesv: Did God leave all humanity to perish in the state of sin and misery?
entries.answer_mesv: Having elected some to everlasting life, out of his mere good pleasure, from all eternity, God entered into a covenant of grace to deliver them out of the state of sin and misery, and to bring them into a state of salvation by a Redeemer.

entries.question: Who is the redeemer of God's elect?
entries.answer: The only redeemer of God's elect is the Lord Jesus Christ, who, being the eternal Son of God, became man, and so was, and continueth to be, God and man in two distinct natures, and one person, forever.
entries.question_mesv: Who is the Redeemer of God's elect?
entries.answer_mesv: The only Redeemer of God's elect is the Lord Jesus Christ, who, being the eternal Son of God, became man, and so was, and continues to be, God and man in two distinct natures, and one person, forever.

entries.question: How did Christ, being the Son of God, become man?
entries.answer: Christ, the Son of God, became man, by taking to himself a true body and a reasonable soul, being conceived by the power of the Holy Ghost in the womb of the virgin Mary, and born of her, yet without sin.
entries.question_mesv: How did Christ, being the Son of God, become man?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ, the Son of God, became man, by taking to himself a true body, and a reasonable soul, being conceived by the power of the Holy Spirit, in the womb of the Virgin Mary, and born of her, yet without sin.

entries.question: What offices doth Christ execute as our redeemer?
entries.answer: Christ, as our redeemer, executeth the offices of a prophet, of a priest, and of a king, both in his estate of humiliation and exaltation.
entries.question_mesv: What offices does Christ execute as our Redeemer?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ, as our Redeemer, executes the offices of a prophet, of a priest, and of a king, both in his state of humiliation and exaltation.

entries.question: How doth Christ execute the office of a prophet?
entries.answer: Christ executeth the office of a prophet, in revealing to us, by his word and Spirit, the will of God for our salvation.
entries.question_mesv: How does Christ execute the office of a prophet?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ executes the office of a prophet, in revealing to us, by his Word and Spirit, the will of God for our salvation.

entries.question: How doth Christ execute the office of a priest?
entries.answer: Christ executeth the office of a priest, in his once offering up of himself a sacrifice to satisfy divine justice, and reconcile us to God; and in making continual intercession for us.
entries.question_mesv: How does Christ execute the office of a priest?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ executes the office of a priest, in his once offering himself up as a sacrifice to satisfy divine justice, and reconcile us to God, and in making continual intercession for us.

entries.question: How doth Christ execute the office of a king?
entries.answer: Christ executeth the office of a king, in subduing us to himself, in ruling and defending us, and in restraining and conquering all his and our enemies.
entries.question_mesv: How does Christ execute the office of a king?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ executes the office of a king, in subduing us to himself, in ruling and defending us, and in restraining and conquering all his and our enemies.

entries.question: Wherein did Christ's humiliation consist?
entries.answer: Christ's humiliation consisted in his being born, and that in a low condition, made under the law, undergoing the miseries of this life, the wrath of God, and the cursed death of the cross; in being buried, and continuing under the power of death for a time.
entries.question_mesv: What was Christ's state of humiliation?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ's humiliation consisted in his being born, and that in a low condition, made under the law, undergoing the miseries of this life, the wrath of God, and the cursed death of the cross; in being buried, and continuing under the power of death for a time.

entries.question: Wherein consisteth Christ's exaltation?
entries.answer: Christ's exaltation consisteth in his rising again from the dead on the third day, in ascending up into heaven, in sitting at the right hand of God the Father, and in coming to judge the world at the last day.
entries.question_mesv: What is Christ's state of exaltation?
entries.answer_mesv: Christ's exaltation consists in his rising from the dead on the third day, in ascending into heaven, in sitting at the right hand of God the Father, and in coming to judge the world at the last day.

entries.question: How are we made partakers of the redemption purchased by Christ?
entries.answer: We are made partakers of the redemption purchased by Christ, by the effectual application of it to us by his Holy Spirit.
entries.question_mesv: How are we made partakers of the redemption purchased by Christ?
entries.answer_mesv: We are made partakers of the redemption purchased by Christ, by the effectual application of it to us by his Holy Spirit.

entries.question: How doth the Spirit apply to us the redemption purchased by Christ?
entries.answer: The Spirit applieth to us the redemption purchased by Christ, by working faith in us, and thereby uniting us to Christ in our effectual calling.
entries.question_mesv: How does the Spirit apply to us the redemption purchased by Christ?
entries.answer_mesv: The Spirit applies to us the redemption purchased by Christ, by working faith in us, and by this uniting us to Christ in our effectual calling.

entries.question: What is effectual calling?
entries.answer: Effectual calling is the work of God's Spirit, whereby, convincing us of our sin and misery, enlightening our minds in the knowledge of Christ, and renewing our wills, he doth persuade and enable us to embrace Jesus Christ, freely offered to us in the gospel.
entries.question_mesv: What is effectual calling?
entries.answer_mesv: Effectual calling is the work of God's Spirit, by which, convincing us of our sin and misery, enlightening our minds in the knowledge of Christ, and renewing our wills, he persuades and enables us to embrace Jesus Christ, freely offered to us in the gospel.

entries.question: What benefits do they that are effectually called partake of in this life?
entries.answer: They that are effectually called do in this life partake of justification, adoption and sanctification, and the several benefits which in this life do either accompany or flow from them.
entries.question_mesv: What benefits do those who are effectually called partake of in this life?
entries.answer_mesv: Those who are effectually called partake in this life of justification, adoption, and sanctification, and the several benefits that in this life either accompany or flow from them.

entries.question: What is justification?
entries.answer: Justification is an act of God's free grace, wherein he pardoneth all our sins, and accepteth us as righteous in his sight, only for the righteousness of Christ imputed to us, and received by faith alone.
entries.question_mesv: What is justification?
entries.answer_mesv: Justification is an act of God's free grace, in which he pardons all our sins, and accepts us as righteous in his sight, only for the righteousness of Christ imputed to us, and received by faith alone.

entries.question: What is adoption?
entries.answer: Adoption is an act of God's free grace, whereby we are received into the number, and have a right to all the privileges of, the sons of God.
entries.question_mesv: What is adoption?
entries.answer_mesv: Adoption is an act of God's free grace, by which we are received into the number, and have a right to all the privileges, of the sons of God.

entries.question: What is sanctification?
entries.answer: Sanctification is the work of God's free grace, whereby we are renewed in the whole man after the image of God, and are enabled more and more to die unto sin, and live unto righteousness.
entries.question_mesv: What is sanctification?
entries.answer_mesv: Sanctification is the work of God's free grace, by which we are renewed in the whole man after the image of God, and are enabled more and more to die to sin, and live to righteousness.

entries.question: What are the benefits which in this life do accompany or flow from justification, adoption and sanctification?
entries.answer: The benefits which in this life do accompany or flow from justification, adoption and sanctification, are, assurance of God's love, peace of conscience, joy in the Holy Ghost, increase of grace, and perseverance therein to the end.
entries.question_mesv: What are the benefits that in this life accompany or flow from justification, adoption, and sanctification?
entries.answer_mesv: The benefits that in this life accompany or flow from justification, adoption, and sanctification, are, assurance of God's love, peace of conscience, joy in the Holy Spirit, increase of grace, and perseverance in it to the end.

entries.question: What benefits do believers receive from Christ at death?
entries.answer: The souls of believers are at their death made perfect in holiness, and do immediately pass into glory; and their bodies, being still united to Christ, do rest in their graves till the resurrection.
entries.question_mesv: What benefits do believers receive from Christ at death?
entries.answer_mesv: The souls of believers are at their death made perfect in holiness, and immediately pass into glory; and their bodies, being still united to Christ, rest in their graves, till the resurrection.

entries.question: What benefits do believers receive from Christ at the resurrection?
entries.answer: At the resurrection, believers, being raised up in glory, shall be openly acknowledged and acquitted in the day of judgment, and made perfectly blessed in the full enjoying of God to all eternity.
entries.question_mesv: What benefits do believers receive from Christ at the resurrection?
entries.answer_mesv: At the resurrection, believers, being raised up in glory, shall be openly acknowledged and acquitted in the day of judgment, and made perfectly blessed in the full enjoying of God to all eternity.

entries.question: What is the duty which God requireth of man?
entries.answer: The duty which God requireth of man is obedience to his revealed will.
entries.question_mesv: What is the duty that God requires of man?
entries.answer_mesv: The duty that God requires of man, is obedience to his revealed will.

entries.question: What did God at first reveal to man for the rule of his obedience?
entries.answer: The rule which God at first revealed to man for his obedience was the moral law.
entries.question_mesv: What did God at first reveal to man for the rule of his obedience?
entries.answer_mesv: The rule that God at first revealed to man for his obedience, was the moral law.

entries.question: Where is the moral law summarily comprehended?
entries.answer: The moral law is summarily comprehended in the ten commandments.
entries.question_mesv: Where is the moral law summarized?
entries.answer_mesv: The moral law is summarized in the Ten Commandments.

entries.question: What is the sum of the ten commandments?
entries.answer: The sum of the ten commandments is to love the Lord our God with all our heart, with all our soul, with all our strength, and with all our mind; and our neighbor as ourselves.
entries.question_mesv: What is the sum of the Ten Commandments?
entries.answer_mesv: The sum of the Ten Commandments is, to love the Lord our God with all our heart, with all our soul, with all our strength, and with all our mind; and our neighbor as ourselves.

entries.question: What is the preface to the ten commandments?
entries.answer: The preface to the ten commandments is in these words, I am the Lord thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.
entries.question_mesv: What is the preface to the Ten Commandments?
entries.answer_mesv: The preface to the Ten Commandments is in these words, I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

entries.question: What doth the preface to the ten commandments teach us?
entries.answer: The preface to the ten commandments teacheth us that because God is the Lord, and our God, and redeemer, therefore we are bound to keep all his commandments.
entries.question_mesv: What does the preface to the Ten Commandments teach us?
entries.answer_mesv: The preface to the Ten Commandments teaches us that because God is the Lord, and our God, and Redeemer, therefore we are bound to keep all his commandments.

entries.question: Which is the first commandment?
entries.answer: The first commandment is, Thou shalt have no other gods before me.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the first commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The first commandment is, You shall have no other gods before me.

entries.question: What is required in the first commandment?
entries.answer: The first commandment requireth us to know and acknowledge God to be the only true God, and our God; and to worship and glorify him accordingly.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the first commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The first commandment requires us to know and acknowledge God to be the only true God, and our God; and to worship and glorify him accordingly.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the first commandment?
entries.answer: The first commandment forbiddeth the denying, or not worshiping and glorifying the true God as God, and our God; and the giving of that worship and glory to any other, which is due to him alone.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the first commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The first commandment forbids denying, or not worshiping and glorifying, the true God as God, and our God; and giving that worship and glory to any other, which is due to him alone.

entries.question: What are we specially taught by these words before me in the first commandment?
entries.answer: These words before me in the first commandment teach us that God, who seeth all things, taketh notice of, and is much displeased with, the sin of having any other god.
entries.question_mesv: What are we specially taught by these words, before me, in the first commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: These words, before me, in the first commandment teach us, that God, who sees all things, takes notice of, and is greatly displeased with, the sin of having any other God.

entries.question: Which is the second commandment?
entries.answer: The second commandment is, Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth: thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the Lord thy God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me; and showing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the second commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The second commandment is, You shall not make for yourself a carved image, or any likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth. You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I the LORD your God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me, but showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.

entries.question: What is required in the second commandment?
entries.answer: The second commandment requireth the receiving, observing, and keeping pure and entire, all such religious worship and ordinances as God hath appointed in his word.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the second commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The second commandment requires receiving, observing, and keeping pure and entire, all the religious worship and ordinances that God has appointed in his Word.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the second commandment?
entries.answer: The second commandment forbiddeth the worshiping of God by images, or any other way not appointed in his word.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the second commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The second commandment forbids worshiping God by images, or any other way not appointed in his Word.

entries.question: What are the reasons annexed to the second commandment?
entries.answer: The reasons annexed to the second commandment are, God's sovereignty over us, his propriety in us, and the zeal he hath to his own worship.
entries.question_mesv: What are the reasons annexed to the second commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The reasons annexed to the second commandment are, God's sovereignty over us, his ownership of us, and the zeal he has for his own worship.

entries.question: Which is the third commandment?
entries.answer: The third commandment is, Thou shalt not take the name of the Lord thy God in vain: for the Lord will not hold him guiltless that taketh his name in vain.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the third commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The third commandment is, You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain, for the LORD will not hold him guiltless who takes his name in vain.

entries.question: What is required in the third commandment?
entries.answer: The third commandment requireth the holy and reverent use of God's names, titles, attributes, ordinances, word and works.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the third commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The third commandment requires the holy and reverent use of God's names, titles, attributes, ordinances, Word, and works.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the third commandment?
entries.answer: The third commandment forbiddeth all profaning or abusing of anything whereby God maketh himself known.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the third commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The third commandment forbids all profaning or abusing of anything by which God makes himself known.

entries.question: What is the reason annexed to the third commandment?
entries.answer: The reason annexed to the third commandment is that however the breakers of this commandment may escape punishment from men, yet the Lord our God will not suffer them to escape his righteous judgment.
entries.question_mesv: What is the reason annexed to the third commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The reason annexed to the third commandment is, that however the breakers of this commandment may escape punishment from men, yet the Lord our God will not allow them to escape his righteous judgment.

entries.question: Which is the fourth commandment?
entries.answer: The fourth commandment is, Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days shalt thou labor, and do all thy work: but the seventh day is the sabbath of the Lord thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter, thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thy cattle, nor thy stranger that is within thy gates: for in six days the Lord made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is, and rested the seventh day: wherefore the Lord blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the fourth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The fourth commandment is, Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days you shall labor, and do all your work, but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the Lord your God. On it you shall not do any work, you, or your son, or your daughter, your male servant, or your female servant, or your livestock, or the sojourner who is within your gates. For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, and rested on the seventh day. Therefore the LORD blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy.

entries.question: What is required in the fourth commandment?
entries.answer: The fourth commandment requireth the keeping holy to God such set times as he hath appointed in his word; expressly one whole day in seven, to be a holy sabbath to himself.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the fourth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The fourth commandment requires keeping holy to God the set times he has appointed in his Word; expressly one whole day in seven, to be a holy Sabbath to himself.

entries.question: Which day of the seven hath God appointed to be the weekly sabbath?
entries.answer: From the beginning of the world to the resurrection of Christ, God appointed the seventh day of the week to be the weekly sabbath; and the first day of the week ever since, to continue to the end of the world, which is the Christian sabbath.
entries.question_mesv: Which day of the seven has God appointed to be the weekly Sabbath?
entries.answer_mesv: From the beginning of the world to the resurrection of Christ, God appointed the seventh day of the week to be the weekly Sabbath; and the first day of the week ever since, to continue to the end of the world, which is the Christian Sabbath.

entries.question: How is the sabbath to be sanctified?
entries.answer: The sabbath is to be sanctified by a holy resting all that day, even from such worldly employments and recreations as are lawful on other days; and spending the whole time in the public and private exercises of God's worship, except so much as is to be taken up in the works of necessity and mercy.
entries.question_mesv: How is the Sabbath to be sanctified?
entries.answer_mesv: The Sabbath is to be sanctified by a holy resting all that day, even from the worldly employments and recreations that are lawful on other days; and spending the whole time in the public and private exercises of God's worship, except so much as is to be taken up in the works of necessity and mercy.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the fourth commandment?
entries.answer: The fourth commandment forbiddeth the omission or careless performance of the duties required, and the profaning the day by idleness, or doing that which is in itself sinful, or by unnecessary thoughts, words or works, about our worldly employments or recreations.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the fourth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The fourth commandment forbids the omission, or careless performance, of the duties required, and profaning the day by idleness, or doing that which is in itself sinful, or by unnecessary thoughts, words, or works, about our worldly employments or recreations.

entries.question: What are the reasons annexed to the fourth commandment?
entries.answer: The reasons annexed to the fourth commandment are, God's allowing us six days of the week for our own employments, his challenging a special propriety in the seventh, his own example, and his blessing the sabbath day.
entries.question_mesv: What are the reasons annexed to the fourth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The reasons annexed to the fourth commandment are, God's allowing us six days of the week for our own employments, his claiming a special ownership of the seventh, his own example, and his blessing the Sabbath day.

entries.question: Which is the fifth commandment?
entries.answer: The fifth commandment is, Honor thy father and thy mother; that thy days may be long upon the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the fifth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The fifth commandment is, Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be long in the land that the LORD your God is giving you.

entries.question: What is required in the fifth commandment?
entries.answer: The fifth commandment requireth the preserving the honor, and performing the duties, belonging to every one in their several places and relations, as superiors, inferiors or equals.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the fifth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The fifth commandment requires preserving the honor, and performing the duties, belonging to everyone in their several places and relations, as superiors, inferiors, or equals.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the fifth commandment?
entries.answer: The fifth commandment forbiddeth the neglecting of, or doing anything against, the honor and duty which belongeth to every one in their several places and relations.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the fifth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The fifth commandment forbids neglecting, or doing anything against, the honor and duty that belongs to everyone in their several places and relations.

entries.question: What is the reason annexed to the fifth commandment?
entries.answer: The reason annexed to the fifth commandment is a promise of long life and prosperity (as far as it shall serve for God's glory and their own good) to all such as keep this commandment.
entries.question_mesv: What is the reason annexed to the fifth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The reason annexed to the fifth commandment is a promise of long life and prosperity (as far as it shall serve for God's glory and their own good) to all who keep this commandment.

entries.question: Which is the sixth commandment?
entries.answer: The sixth commandment is, Thou shalt not kill.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the sixth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The sixth commandment is, You shall not murder.

entries.question: What is required in the sixth commandment?
entries.answer: The sixth commandment requireth all lawful endeavors to preserve our own life, and the life of others.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the sixth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The sixth commandment requires all lawful endeavors to preserve our own life, and the life of others.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the sixth commandment?
entries.answer: The sixth commandment forbiddeth the taking away of our own life, or the life of our neighbor unjustly, or whatsoever tendeth thereunto.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the sixth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The sixth commandment forbids taking away our own life, or the life of our neighbor unjustly, or whatever tends to it.

entries.question: Which is the seventh commandment?
entries.answer: The seventh commandment is, Thou shalt not commit adultery.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the seventh commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The seventh commandment is, You shall not commit adultery.

entries.question: What is required in the seventh commandment?
entries.answer: The seventh commandment requireth the preservation of our own and our neighbor's chastity, in heart, speech and behavior.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the seventh commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The seventh commandment requires the preservation of our own and our neighbor's chastity, in heart, speech, and behavior.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the seventh commandment?
entries.answer: The seventh commandment forbiddeth all unchaste thoughts, words and actions.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the seventh commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The seventh commandment forbids all unchaste thoughts, words, and actions.

entries.question: Which is the eighth commandment?
entries.answer: The eighth commandment is, Thou shalt not steal.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the eighth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The eighth commandment is, You shall not steal.

entries.question: What is required in the eighth commandment?
entries.answer: The eighth commandment requireth the lawful procuring and furthering the wealth and outward estate of ourselves and others.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the eighth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The eighth commandment requires the lawful procuring and furthering of the wealth and outward estate of ourselves and others.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the eighth commandment?
entries.answer: The eighth commandment forbiddeth whatsoever doth or may unjustly hinder our own or our neighbor's wealth or outward estate.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the eighth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The eighth commandment forbids whatever does, or may, unjustly hinder our own, or our neighbor's, wealth or outward estate.

entries.question: Which is the ninth commandment?
entries.answer: The ninth commandment is, Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbor.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the ninth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The ninth commandment is, You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor.

entries.question: What is required in the ninth commandment?
entries.answer: The ninth commandment requireth the maintaining and promoting of truth between man and man, and of our own and our neighbor's good name, especially in witness-bearing.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the ninth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The ninth commandment requires maintaining and promoting truth between man and man, and of our own and our neighbor's good name, especially in witness bearing.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the ninth commandment?
entries.answer: The ninth commandment forbiddeth whatsoever is prejudicial to truth, or injurious to our own or our neighbor's good name.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the ninth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The ninth commandment forbids whatever is prejudicial to truth, or injurious to our own, or our neighbor's, good name.

entries.question: Which is the tenth commandment?
entries.answer: The tenth commandment is, Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's house, thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor anything that is thy neighbor's.
entries.question_mesv: Which is the tenth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The tenth commandment is, You shall not covet your neighbor's house; you shall not covet your neighbor's wife, or his male servant, or his female servant, or his ox, or his donkey, or anything that is your neighbor's.

entries.question: What is required in the tenth commandment?
entries.answer: The tenth commandment requireth full contentment with our own condition, with a right and charitable frame of spirit toward our neighbor, and all that is his.
entries.question_mesv: What is required in the tenth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The tenth commandment requires full contentment with our own condition, with a right and charitable disposition toward our neighbor, and all that is his.

entries.question: What is forbidden in the tenth commandment?
entries.answer: The tenth commandment forbiddeth all discontentment with our own estate, envying or grieving at the good of our neighbor, and all inordinate motions and affections to anything that is his.
entries.question_mesv: What is forbidden in the tenth commandment?
entries.answer_mesv: The tenth commandment forbids all discontentment with our own condition, envying or grieving at the good of our neighbor, and all inordinate motions and affections to anything that is his.

entries.question: Is any man able perfectly to keep the commandments of God?
entries.answer: No mere man since the fall is able in this life perfectly to keep the commandments of God, but doth daily break them in thought, word and deed.
entries.question_mesv: Is any man able to keep the commandments of God perfectly?
entries.answer_mesv: No mere man since the fall, is able in this life to keep the commandments of God perfectly, but daily breaks them in thought, word, and deed.

entries.question: Are all transgressions of the law equally heinous?
entries.answer: Some sins in themselves, and by reason of several aggravations, are more heinous in the sight of God than others.
entries.question_mesv: Are all transgressions of the law equally heinous?
entries.answer_mesv: Some sins in themselves, and by reason of several aggravations, are more heinous in the sight of God than others.

entries.question: What doth every sin deserve?
entries.answer: Every sin deserveth God's wrath and curse, both in this life, and that which is to come.
entries.question_mesv: What does every sin deserve?
entries.answer_mesv: Every sin deserves God's wrath and curse, both in this life, and that which is to come.

entries.question: What doth God require of us that we may escape his wrath and curse due to us for sin?
entries.answer: To escape the wrath and curse of God due to us for sin, God requireth of us faith in Jesus Christ, repentance unto life, with the diligent use of all the outward means whereby Christ communicateth to us the benefits of redemption.
entries.question_mesv: What does God require of us, that we may escape his wrath and curse, due to us for sin?
entries.answer_mesv: To escape the wrath and curse of God, due to us for sin, God requires of us faith in Jesus Christ and repentance unto life, with the diligent use of all the outward means by which Christ communicates to us the benefits of redemption.

entries.question: What is faith in Jesus Christ?
entries.answer: Faith in Jesus Christ is a saving grace, whereby we receive and rest upon him alone for salvation, as he is offered to us in the gospel.
entries.question_mesv: What is faith in Jesus Christ?
entries.answer_mesv: Faith in Jesus Christ is a saving grace, by which we receive and rest upon him alone for salvation, as he is offered to us in the gospel.

entries.question: What is repentance unto life?
entries.answer: Repentance unto life is a saving grace, whereby a sinner, out of a true sense of his sin, and apprehension of the mercy of God in Christ, doth, with grief and hatred of his sin, turn from it unto God, with full purpose of, and endeavor after, new obedience.
entries.question_mesv: What is repentance unto life?
entries.answer_mesv: Repentance unto life is a saving grace, by which a sinner, out of a true sense of his sin, and apprehension of the mercy of God in Christ, with grief and hatred of his sin, turns from it to God, with full purpose of, and endeavor after, new obedience.

entries.question: What are the outward and ordinary means whereby Christ communicateth to us the benefits of redemption?
entries.answer: The outward and ordinary means whereby Christ communicateth to us the benefits of redemption, are his ordinances, especially the word, sacraments, and prayer; all which are made effectual to the elect for salvation.
entries.question_mesv: What are the outward and ordinary means by which Christ communicates to us the benefits of redemption?
entries.answer_mesv: The outward and ordinary means by which Christ communicates to us the benefits of redemption are, his ordinances, especially the word, sacraments, and prayer; all of which are made effectual to the elect for salvation.

entries.question: How is the word made effectual to salvation?
entries.answer: The Spirit of God maketh the reading, but especially the preaching, of the word, an effectual means of convincing and converting sinners, and of building them up in holiness and comfort, through faith, unto salvation.
entries.question_mesv: How is the Word made effectual to salvation?
entries.answer_mesv: The Spirit of God makes the reading, but especially the preaching, of the Word, an effectual means of convincing and converting sinners, and of building them up in holiness and comfort, through faith, to salvation.

entries.question: How is the word to be read and heard, that it may become effectual to salvation?
entries.answer: That the word may become effectual to salvation, we must attend thereunto with diligence, preparation and prayer; receive it with faith and love, lay it up in our hearts, and practice it in our lives.
entries.question_mesv: How is the Word to be read and heard, that it may become effectual to salvation?
entries.answer_mesv: That the Word may become effectual to salvation, we must attend to it with diligence, preparation, and prayer; receive it with faith and love, lay it up in our hearts, and practice it in our lives.

entries.question: How do the sacraments become effectual means of salvation?
entries.answer: The sacraments become effectual means of salvation, not from any virtue in them, or in him that doth administer them; but only by the blessing of Christ, and the working of his Spirit in them that by faith receive them.
entries.question_mesv: How do the sacraments become effectual means of salvation?
entries.answer_mesv: The sacraments become effectual means of salvation, not from any virtue in them, or in him that administers them; but only by the blessing of Christ, and the working of his Spirit in those who by faith receive them.

entries.question: What is a sacrament?
entries.answer: A sacrament is an holy ordinance instituted by Christ; wherein, by sensible signs, Christ, and the benefits of the new covenant, are represented, sealed, and applied to believers.
entries.question_mesv: What is a sacrament?
entries.answer_mesv: A sacrament is a holy ordinance instituted by Christ; in which, by sensible1 signs, Christ, and the benefits of the new covenant, are represented, sealed, and applied to believers.

entries.question: Which are the sacraments of the New Testament?
entries.answer: The sacraments of the New Testament are baptism and the Lord's supper.
entries.question_mesv: Which are the sacraments of the New Testament?
entries.answer_mesv: The sacraments of the New Testament are, baptism, and the Lord's Supper.

entries.question: What is baptism?
entries.answer: Baptism is a sacrament, wherein the washing with water in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost, doth signify and seal our ingrafting into Christ, and partaking of the benefits of the covenant of grace, and our engagement to be the Lord's.
entries.question_mesv: What is baptism?
entries.answer_mesv: Baptism is a sacrament, in which the washing with water in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit, signifies and seals our ingrafting into Christ, and partaking of the benefits of the covenant of grace, and our engagement to be the Lord's.

entries.question: To whom is baptism to be administered?
entries.answer: Baptism is not to be administered to any that are out of the visible church, till they profess their faith in Christ, and obedience to him; but the infants of such as are members of the visible church are to be baptized.
entries.question_mesv: To whom is baptism to be administered?
entries.answer_mesv: Baptism is not to be administered to any that are out of the visible church, till they profess their faith in Christ, and obedience to him; but the infants of members of the visible church are to be baptized.

entries.question: What is the Lord's supper?
entries.answer: The Lord's supper is a sacrament, wherein, by giving and receiving bread and wine according to Christ's appointment, his death is showed forth; and the worthy receivers are, not after a corporal and carnal manner, but by faith, made partakers of his body and blood, with all his benefits, to their spiritual nourishment and growth in grace.
entries.question_mesv: What is the Lord's Supper?
entries.answer_mesv: The Lord's Supper is a sacrament, in which, by giving and receiving bread and wine, according to Christ's appointment, his death is shown forth; and the worthy receivers are, not in a corporal and carnal manner, but by faith, made partakers of his body and blood, with all his benefits, to their spiritual nourishment, and growth in grace.

entries.question: What is required to the worthy receiving of the Lord's supper?
entries.answer: It is required of them that would worthily partake of the Lord's supper, that they examine themselves of their knowledge to discern the Lord's body, of their faith to feed upon him, of their repentance, love, and new obedience; lest, coming unworthily, they eat and drink judgment to themselves.
entries.question_mesv: What is required for the worthy receiving of the Lord's Supper?
entries.answer_mesv: It is required of those who would worthily partake of the Lord's Supper, that they examine themselves of their knowledge to discern the Lord's body, of their faith to feed upon him, of their repentance, love, and new obedience; lest, coming unworthily, they eat and drink judgment to themselves.

entries.question: What is prayer?
entries.answer: Prayer is an offering up of our desires unto God, for things agreeable to his will, in the name of Christ, with confession of our sins, and thankful acknowledgment of his mercies.
entries.question_mesv: What is prayer?
entries.answer_mesv: Prayer is an offering up of our desires to God, for things agreeable to his will, in the name of Christ, with confession of our sins, and thankful acknowledgment of his mercies. 1 Sensible means they can be sensed by the five senses: sight, hearing, feeling, taste, and smell.

entries.question: What rule hath God given for our direction in prayer?
entries.answer: The whole word of God is of use to direct us in prayer; but the special rule of direction is that form of prayer which Christ taught his disciples, commonly called the Lord's prayer.
entries.question_mesv: What rule has God given for our direction in prayer?
entries.answer_mesv: The whole Word of God is of use to direct us in prayer; but the special rule of direction is that form of prayer which Christ taught his disciples, commonly called the Lord's Prayer.

entries.question: What doth the preface of the Lord's prayer teach us?
entries.answer: The preface of the Lord's prayer, which is, Our Father which art in heaven, teacheth us to draw near to God with all holy reverence and confidence, as children to a father able and ready to help us; and that we should pray with and for others.
entries.question_mesv: What does the preface of the Lord's Prayer teach us?
entries.answer_mesv: The preface of the Lord's Prayer, which is, Our Father in heaven, teaches us to draw near to God with all holy reverence and confidence, as children to a father, able and ready to help us; and that we should pray with and for others.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the first petition?
entries.answer: In the first petition, which is, Hallowed be thy name, we pray that God would enable us and others to glorify him in all that whereby he maketh himself known; and that he would dispose all things to his own glory.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the first petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the first petition, which is, Hallowed be your name, we pray that God would enable us, and others, to glorify him in everything by which he makes himself known; and that he would cause all things to work for his own glory.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the second petition?
entries.answer: In the second petition, which is, Thy kingdom come, we pray that Satan's kingdom may be destroyed; and that the kingdom of grace may be advanced, ourselves and others brought into it, and kept in it; and that the kingdom of glory may be hastened.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the second petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the second petition, which is, Your kingdom come, we pray that Satan's kingdom may be destroyed; and that the kingdom of grace may be advanced, ourselves and others brought into it, and kept in it; and that the kingdom of glory may be hastened.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the third petition?
entries.answer: In the third petition, which is, Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven, we pray that God, by his grace, would make us able and willing to know, obey and submit to his will in all things, as the angels do in heaven.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the third petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the third petition, which is, Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven, we pray that God, by his grace, would make us able and willing to know, obey, and submit to his will in all things, as the angels do in heaven.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the fourth petition?
entries.answer: In the fourth petition, which is, Give us this day our daily bread, we pray that of God's free gift we may receive a competent portion of the good things of this life, and enjoy his blessing with them.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the fourth petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the fourth petition, which is, Give us this day our daily bread, we pray that of God's free gift, we may receive a fitting portion of the good things of this life, and enjoy his blessing with them.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the fifth petition?
entries.answer: In the fifth petition, which is, And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors, we pray that God, for Christ's sake, would freely pardon all our sins; which we are the rather encouraged to ask, because by his grace we are enabled from the heart to forgive others.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the fifth petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the fifth petition, which is, And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors, we pray that God, for Christ's sake, would freely pardon all our sins; which we are encouraged to ask more readily, because by his grace we are enabled from the heart to forgive others.

entries.question: What do we pray for in the sixth petition?
entries.answer: In the sixth petition, which is, And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil, we pray that God would either keep us from being tempted to sin, or support and deliver us when we are tempted.
entries.question_mesv: What do we pray for in the sixth petition?
entries.answer_mesv: In the sixth petition, which is, And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil, we pray that God would either keep us from being tempted to sin, or support and deliver us when we are tempted.

entries.question: What doth the conclusion of the Lord's prayer teach us?
entries.answer: The conclusion of the Lord's prayer, which is, For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever, Amen, teacheth us to take our encouragement in prayer from God only, and in our prayers to praise him, ascribing kingdom, power and glory to him. And in testimony of our desire, and assurance to be heard, we say, Amen.
entries.question_mesv: What does the conclusion of the Lord's Prayer teach us?
entries.answer_mesv: The conclusion of the Lord's Prayer, which is, For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory, forever. Amen, teaches us to take our encouragement in prayer from God only, and in our prayers to praise him, ascribing kingdom, power, and glory to him; and, in testimony of our desire, and assurance to be heard, we say, Amen.
